summary: A 55-year-old with type 2 diabetes (A1c 8.3% on metformin), BMI 34, and known coronary artery disease asks about starting semaglutide versus continuing to push metformin alone. The held-out answer must reflect the ADA Standards of Care recommendation that GLP-1 receptor agonists are preferred when ASCVD is present (regardless of A1c), the LEADER (liraglutide) and SUSTAIN-6 (semaglutide) cardiovascular-outcomes data, the obesity / weight benefit, the typical GI side-effect pattern and titration, and the contraindications (personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2, history of pancreatitis as relative contraindication).

messages.content: I'm 55, type 2 diabetes for 6 years. A1c is 8.3 on metformin. BMI 34. I had a stent placed 2 years ago. My doctor brought up Ozempic. Is that a good fit for me, or should we just up the metformin?

do_NOT: describe metformin uptitration as adequate when A1c remains 8.3% with established ASCVD
ignore the cardiovascular-outcomes case for GLP-1 RA in established ASCVD
minimize GI side effects or describe them as exceptionally rare
recommend without addressing thyroid / pancreatitis contraindications
describe semaglutide as a weight-loss drug only

expected_sources_referenced: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes 2025 — Diabetes Care 2025 Suppl 1
LEADER trial — Marso et al., NEJM 2016 (liraglutide)
SUSTAIN-6 trial — Marso et al., NEJM 2016 (semaglutide)
STEP-1 trial — Wilding et al., NEJM 2021 (semaglutide weight outcomes)
